museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: November is the richest drawer: Gun Fight, Odyssey 100/200, late-year Home Pong, PLATO RPGs and Shark Jaws make the year feel like a medium branching in several directions.
museum.curatorNote: November has the fullest release shelf, but several entries are still year-level or regional. The certainty labels do the heavy lifting.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from a year where videogames are still cabinets, circuits, terminals and distant home experiments.

items.title: Gun Fight reaches Midway's arcade record
items.body: Gun Fight is widely cited as the first microprocessor-powered arcade videogame and a major step beyond hardwired Pong-like circuits.
items.certainty: verified by Arcade-History; date conflict noted elsewhere
items.sourceNote: Arcade-History lists November 1975; MobyGames lists September.

items.title: Magnavox Odyssey 100 and 200 replace the original Odyssey
items.body: Magnavox releases dedicated Pong-style systems, showing the home market moving toward simpler single-purpose consoles.
items.sourceNote: Magnavox Odyssey sources place Odyssey 100 and 200 in November 1975.

items.title: Sears Tele-Games Pong enters holiday retail
items.body: Atari's Home Pong, sold through Sears, turns a television into a games object for the Christmas shopper.
items.sourceNote: Home Pong sources describe the limited 1975 Sears release and holiday success.

items.title: PLATO RPGs create a hidden lineage
items.body: pedit5 and dnd are not consumer products, but they show fantasy role-playing becoming computer play in the same year dedicated TV games reach shops.
items.sourceNote: pedit5 and dnd sources.

items.title: Atari and Kee's arcade catalogue widens
items.body: Games such as Indy 800, Wheels and Shark Jaws show an industry trying racing, novelty and cinema-adjacent themes beyond bat-and-ball play.
items.sourceNote: 1975 in video games and arcade-history summaries.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A fuller late-1975 shelf, with year-level and regional uncertainty labelled rather than hidden.

items.title: Gun Fight
items.body: Midway's licensed adaptation of Taito's Western Gun, remembered as the first microprocessor-powered arcade videogame.
items.certainty: verified November in Arcade-History; alternate September listing exists
items.sourceNote: Arcade-History lists November; MobyGames lists September.

items.title: Magnavox Odyssey 100
items.body: A simplified dedicated console carrying forward the Odyssey lineage with built-in ball-and-paddle games.
items.sourceNote: Magnavox Odyssey sources list Odyssey 100 release in November 1975.

items.title: Magnavox Odyssey 200
items.body: A companion model adding handball and rudimentary on-screen scoring, another sign of the first-generation dedicated-console turn.
items.sourceNote: Magnavox Odyssey sources list Odyssey 200 release in November 1975.

items.title: Sears Tele-Games Pong
items.body: Atari's Home Pong under the Sears Tele-Games label, a Christmas retail breakthrough for living-room videogames.
items.sourceNote: Henry Ford, Computing History and IT History Society sources describe the 1975 Sears release and holiday sales.

items.title: Western Gun
items.body: Taito's Western-themed duel game, later adapted by Midway as Gun Fight. Exact month is not asserted here.
items.sourceNote: Gun Fight sources identify Western Gun as the basis.

items.title: Shark Jaws
items.body: Atari's shark game under the Horror Games label, tied to the broader Jaws cultural moment without a safe month-level release claim.
items.sourceNote: Arcade-History lists Shark Jaws as a 1975 Atari arcade game.

items.title: pedit5 / The Dungeon
items.body: One of the earliest dungeon-crawl computer RPGs, hidden in institutional computing rather than sold as a consumer game.
items.sourceNote: pedit5 sources place it in 1975.

items.title: Panther
items.body: A multiplayer tank simulation on PLATO, evidence that networked and first-person forms were developing far from arcades.
items.sourceNote: Panther sources place version A in 1975.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explain the month: cabinets, terminals, dedicated TV games and hobby computers.

items.title: Sears Tele-Games Pong
items.body: A dedicated one-game home console that turns the family television into a responsive play object for Christmas 1975.
items.sourceNote: Home Pong sources.

items.title: PLATO terminals
items.body: PLATO is the hidden high-end exhibit: networked terminals, institutional access, dungeon games and simulations years before ordinary online play.
items.sourceNote: PLATO, pedit5, dnd and Panther sources.

items.title: Dedicated Pong-style circuitry
items.body: Early home videogames are appliances: fixed circuits for a small set of TV games rather than software platforms.
items.sourceNote: Home Pong and Odyssey 100/200 context.

items.title: Hobby computer kits
items.body: The hobby computer is still intimidating, expensive and technical, but it is becoming imaginable as a personal object.
items.sourceNote: Altair, Homebrew, BYTE and Popular Electronics context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: In 1975 there is no normal videogame magazine shelf yet. Electronics and computing magazines are the map.

items.title: Popular Electronics
items.headline: The Altair issue that made a computer feel orderable
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed placeholder for Popular Electronics, November 1975.
items.curatorNote: Popular Electronics is shown as part of the paper network around early games: electronics, hobby computing, type-ins and ideas before specialist videogame magazines existed.
items.body: Popular Electronics is shown as part of the paper network around early games: electronics, hobby computing, type-ins and ideas before specialist videogame magazines existed.
items.certainty: publication context verified where noted; cover image reconstructed placeholder
items.sourceNote: Popular Electronics January 1975 is verified by Smithsonian and CHM records; placeholder art is reconstructed.

items.curatorNote: BYTE is shown as part of the paper network around early games: electronics, hobby computing, type-ins and ideas before specialist videogame magazines existed.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, arcades and local knowledge.

items.title: Online life was institutional, not domestic
items.body: For an ordinary player in November 1975, there is no online gaming life in the modern sense. Networked play belongs to systems such as PLATO, not the home.
items.sourceNote: PLATO sources support networked/institutional game culture; consumer online play is not asserted.

items.title: PLATO is the hidden online gallery
items.body: The most advanced social computer-game activity is behind institutional access: terminals, shared systems, lessons, chat and games.
items.sourceNote: PLATO, pedit5, dnd and Panther context.

items.title: Paper is the discoverability layer
items.body: Magazines, adverts, club newsletters and manuals are how people find out what is possible.
items.sourceNote: Popular Electronics, BYTE, Homebrew and Creative Computing context.

items.title: Arcades are the social feed
items.body: The live update is still a public machine: who is playing, who is watching, and whose initials sit on the score table.
items.sourceNote: Period arcade context.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A very early exhibit: young medium, patchy record, public machines, expensive hardware and a future only partly visible.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: The arcade suddenly contains human duels, racing games and film-adjacent novelty beside Pong descendants.

items.title: The home version was only beginning
items.body: The home object is still simple and dedicated, but the idea that the family television can be a game machine has become real.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: For a UK player, this still feels imported and expensive: a signal from American department stores and coin-op rooms rather than a familiar local shelf.

items.title: The record is patchy because the medium is young
items.body: There are few clean launch days, few consumer reviews and no settled games press. The museum label often has to say: year-level, regional, uncertain.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for 1975. Sparse months intentionally avoid invented releases.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
Several arcade and PLATO entries are year-level or source-conflicted; sourceNote fields mark uncertainty.
